fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

WILSON J

Today (10 March 2026) is the sixth day of the trial of this parenting proceeding. Shortly after 10am today, counsel for the ICL applied for orders that I recuse myself as the trial judge, or if that was not successful, for the trial to be adjourned sine die to enable the ICL to apply for leave to appeal against those orders.
When it was pointed out to counsel for the ICL that his role was to advance the best interests of the child and that the mother had the main carriage of the recusal application, counsel for the mother rose to apply for orders that I recuse myself.
Counsel for the mother advanced extremely imprecise submissions in support of the recusal application. As best I was able to construe them, her contentions amounted to the following –
(a) my questioning of the mother about the mother’s ongoing involvement with the father took the form of apprehended bias because a fair-minded observer might take the view that I might not bring an impartial mind to the adjudication of this proceeding; and

(b) my questioning of the mother about why she did not leave the factory amounted to apprehended bias because a fair-minded observer might take the view that I might not bring an impartial mind to the adjudication of this proceeding.

Counsel for the ICL submitted that my questioning of the mother revealed bias against women generally.
Before going to the specific exchanges which are impugned on this recusal application, it is necessary to record certain legal principles concerning apprehended bias. They may be catalogued in the manner that now follows.
In Charisteas v Charisteas[1] the High Court of Australia held that the apprehension of bias principles give effect to the requirement that justice should not only be done and that it should be seen to be done, reflecting a requirement fundamental to the common law system of adversarial trial – that it is conducted by an independent and impartial tribunal. That statement reflected earlier statements to like effect in Ebner v Official Trustee in Bankruptcy[2] and in Concrete Pty Ltd v Parramatta Design & Developments Pty Ltd.[3]
The application of the principle just recorded involves two steps, as the High Court held in Ebner and in Charisteas. The first requires the identification of what it is said that might lead a judge to decide a case other than on its legal and factual merits. The second requires an articulation of a logical connection between the first matter and the feared departure from the judge deciding the case on the merits. Ebner stands for the second proposition as the High Court held in Charisteas.
The reasonableness of any asserted apprehension of bias is to be considered in the context of ordinary judicial practice as the High Court held in Johnson v Johnson[4] in Concrete[5] and in Charisteas.[6]
Next, pursuant to s 102NE(4) of the Family Law Act, in a child-related proceeding the court is to actively direct, control and manage the conduct of the proceeding.
Next, it is a cardinal principle of litigation that parties are bound by the conduct of their counsel, as was held in Nudd v R.[7] To that end, where it can be seen that a failure to object was a rational, tactical decision, the court is entitled to conclude that no unfairness attended the process, as was held in Suresh v R[8] and in Patel v R.[9] When questions are put to one party’s witness, the court is entitled to expect that counsel representing that witness will take issue by objecting to a specific question or to a line of questioning or to a general approach being adopted in the tenor and import of that questioning. In this case, counsel for the mother as well as counsel for the ICL failed to object in any shape or form to any question on which they now rely on this recusal application.
It is necessary to go to the precise minutiae of the grounds on which this recusal application is based.
During the course of the mother’s questioning on the fifth day of the trial, counsel for the father was pressing the mother about her evidence in paragraph 33 of her trial affidavit. The mother answered questions from counsel for the father to the effect that her pregnancy with the child was not planned. She deposed to no longer wanting to be in a relationship with the father when she became pregnant. She gave viva voce evidence to the effect that she did not discuss with the father having a child. The mother said, in substance that she did not break up with the father upon discovering that she was pregnant because she was scared. Counsel for the father pressed the mother about her wanting to keep the baby. The mother said in substance, that she did not plan on becoming pregnant because she could not take a life so she tried to do what was best for her baby. I then sought clarification of her answer and I asked (in an open manner) whether it crossed her mind that she probably would have ongoing contact with the father if she retained the pregnancy. She answered in the affirmative. Slightly later she answered a second time in the affirmative to that proposition.
Counsel for the mother submitted that the exchange summarised immediately above raised an inference that I (as the trial judge) did not accept the mother’s evidence that she had been subjected to family violence. Counsel for the mother went further by submitting that I was to be taken to have demonstrated a rejection of her evidence of family violence by reason of the fact that I had (allegedly) taken the view that the mother chose not to have an abortion.
Counsel for the ICL did not develop his argument that I had exhibited a bias against women generally. Counsel for the ICL did not develop submissions of the sort rendered essential by the High Court’s decision in Charisteas that –
(a) the party asserting the existence of apprehended bias must identify what it is said might lead a judge to decide the case other than on its legal and factual merits; and

(b) the party asserting the existence of apprehended bias must articulate the logical connection between the asserted bias and the feared departure from the judge deciding the case on its merits.

During debate I pointed out to all counsel that the father and his parents had given evidence and that the mother was still in the course of giving her evidence. I said that she had not completed her evidence. Other witnesses for the mother are yet to be called. Expert evidenced is yet to be adduced. The trial has several days to run.
On behalf of the father, counsel submitted it was perfectly proper for me to ask the questions I did as have been summarised above. He submitted that no inference (at least not of the sort canvassed in such cases as Holloway v McFeeters[10]or Luxton v Vines[11]) could be drawn from those questions that I was critical in any way of the mother. He submitted that my questions followed questions by him about the mother’s dissatisfaction with the relationship.
Counsel for the father submitted that it was significant that no objection was taken by counsel for the ICL or for the mother protesting about my questions.
In my view, the first basis on which the ICL and the mother rely for their recusal application must be dismissed. I say that for several reasons. First, they did not object to the questions when put. The mother answered them. She gave her evidence freely and in an unrestrained way. The failure of counsel for the mother and for the ICL to object meant that they had made a rational and tactical decision by not objecting and that I am entitled to conclude that those questions were not attended by any unfairness or bias.
The next reason why I take the view that under the first ground no apprehension of bias existed was that the ICL and the mother failed –
(a) to articulate the precise way it was said that what I did when asking the mother the questions set out above might lead me to decide the case other than on its legal and factual merits; and

(b) to articulate the logical connection between the exchange of questions I engaged in and the feared departure from my deciding the case on the merits.

Other than couching submissions in very vague terms, the necessary precision in making out a claim to apprehended bias was wholly sidestepped and bypassed.
In my view, the challenge based on apprehended bias failed on the first ground.
The second ground asserted was more ephemeral. It arose in the context of questions put to the mother to the effect that she had lived for a time with the child in her parents’ home and that that she moved into a factory occupied by the father, leaving the relative comfort of her parents’ home. The mother gave evidence that upon learning that she was pregnant her relationship with the father had deteriorated and he became abusive. She said she did not insist on her mother being present because she (the respondent in this case) was scared of Mr Ashdown (the applicant in this case). The mother gave evidence that at the insistence of the father, she and the child left the mother’s family home where she was happy and the mother and child moved into a factory occupied by the father. I asked the mother to help me understand why she left the nurturing and comforting environment of her parents’ home to move into a factory with a person between whom things were terrible. She said it was foolish of her to do so. I then adjourned the court for lunch to enable the mother to have a break.
Counsel for the mother relied on her contentions in respect of the first ground in support of her assertion of the existence of apprehended bias on the second ground. No new or additional submissions were advanced by the ICL or the mother in respect of the second ground. Counsel for the father relied on the same grounds under the second ground as were relied on under the first ground.
A debate emerged about whether the reference in my questioning to “leaving” by the mother was a reference to her leaving the factory or whether it was a reference to her leaving her relationship with the father. The point became academic because my question of the mother was why she left her parents’ home to move into the factory with the father. She said the father told her things would get better and that she believed him. I asked why she believed him. She volunteered it was foolish of her.
I am unable to see how that enquiry formed the basis of a recusal application for apprehended bias. The double might test as espoused in Johnson v Johnson,[12] Concrete Pty Ltd v Parramatta Design & Developments Pty Ltd,[13] Ebner v Official Trustee in Bankruptcy[14] and Charisteas v Charisteas[15] was not engaged. Moreover, counsel for the mother and for the ICL could have but failed to object. They did so with the consequence that no unfairness was enlivened by the exchange with the mother.
I refuse to recuse myself for apprehended bias.
Counsel for the ICL submitted that even if I was against the recusal application, the mother and the ICL asked for this matter to be adjourned sine die to enable those parties to apply for leave to appeal. They gave no time estimate of when they may be able to file the relevant papers to bring their application for leave before a Full Court as a matter of urgency.
The father opposed that application by the ICL and by the mother. He submitted as follows –
(a) the evidence is incomplete;

(b) experts are yet to give evidence;

(c) it may well transpire that after trial the mother succeeds on her application for the father to have no time with the child;

(d) the father (and for that matter all parties) have been put to very considerable cost in bringing this case to trial, which will be delayed still further if an interlocutory application for leave to appeal is heard;

(e) the proceeding has been on foot since 2022;

(f) it is not in the child’s best interests to prolong the case further;

(g) the whole point of the curial intervention was to test the mother’s evidence;

(h) a greater injustice will befall the child if this proceeding is halted, now that it is on its sixth day of trial, to enable the mother and the ICL to apply in due course for leave to appeal against my refusal to recuse myself.

Counsel for the ICL did not formulate any proposal in the event I rejected his and the mother’s recusal application.
It is undesirable for the trial of this proceeding to be left in a state of suspended animation as a consequence of my refusing the recusal application brought by the wife and by the ICL. If the mother and the ICL believe on legally maintainable grounds that they have a proper basis to seek the intervention of the Full Court, they should have a short time to file an application before the Full Court seeking leave to appeal. At present no application has been brought. But it is unrealistic for them to take the view that they should have as long as they wish to file their application for leave to appeal and that this trial comes to a grinding halt until they not only file their leave application but that application is determined adversely to the father who wants to keep this trial ongoing.
In addition, it cannot be ignored that a possibility exists that I may accede to the mother’s and ICL’s request to adjourn the trial sine die and that the mother and the ICL do not take expeditious steps to progress any application for leave to appeal against these orders.
The trial of this proceeding has Wednesday to Friday this week to run plus one day next week. That time should not be lost if the mother and the ICL do not seek leave to appeal.
Accordingly, I am willing to adjourn the trial of this proceeding until 2.15pm on Thursday 12 March 2026 by which time the trial will resume unless the mother and the ICL have filed by then an application returnable before the Full Court seeking –
(a) orders staying my decision in these reasons; and

(b) orders for the grant of leave to appeal against these reasons.

All associated documents must also be filed by that date including any affidavit in support of the application for leave to appeal.
If no such application has been filed by 2.15pm on Thursday 12 March 2026, the trial will resume.
